# Transcrição: TBT Week 8 Teleclass

- **Fonte:** TBT Week 8 Teleclass
- **Duração:** 1:40:33
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome to our total breakthrough training session. We are going to be talking today, this is Mark and Magalie, and we are going to be talking today about how to work with two or more clients at a time. So either you're working with a couple, or you're working with two people in a family, or two people who are in a work relationship, any situation where you have a triangle between yourself as a practitioner and two other people. In most cases, you are looking to create some kind of agreement between the two of them. And so we have here for you some steps, some easy steps for handling that dynamic. Magalie, would you like to... Absolutely, Mark, if you could... Start at the slide? Yep. Absolutely, thank you. So one of the most important things to think about before you start your work with either a couple or let's say two business partners or a parent and a child is what is the reason that they're coming to coaching for? Usually, there's just two reasons, and that's either because of a conflict or problem as reason number one, or team building. So this could be in a marriage to increase passion. It could be for a parent and child to get along better or for coworkers or so many different types of relationships where people come to coaching together in order to increase the communication, the strengths of the relationship. But one thing that I think is really important to remember is that even when clients come to you because of a conflict or a problem they're experiencing, as the coach, what needs to happen is that you increase their ability to work together as a team. And this is part of healing the conflict or the problem. It's never really either or. So however they're presenting, the bigger purpose as a coach is to help that relationship strengthen. And then through the strength, often the conflict can be resolved in a way that really works for both people that would not have happened if they didn't have the newer strength that you help them achieve through the coaching relationship. Exactly. And so often you'll have someone, a couple or people who are coming in and they have a conflict over something that's kind of like a... they will often focus on something that looks like a dilemma, like do we spend this money or do we not spend this money? Do you do this? Do you take this job or do you not take this job? Who's going to handle this project? Who's going to do this work? And it's about the vehicle and what you need to keep in mind.

## [03:03 - 06:03]

And the vehicle, sometimes that problem, that is a difficult decision to call. But underlying the tension and the charge that comes from the conflict, you have to look for the needs underneath it. And basically you'll quickly be able to pick up that there are some needs that are out being met. And those are the ones that you want to focus on. And so when you're working with two people, you're also coordinating their needs so that they are able to see each other's needs and meet them. And then you're basically crafting an agreement between them and not only an agreement in the sense of, you know, you capture them in an agreement state, but getting them to really see what is underneath it and how they can join as a team. So when we're joining as a team and making feelings like that, how the person has your back, and that they will meet their needs. And likewise, they should want to meet their needs. So if we move on now to the next slide, Mark. Yep. This is five keys to success. And so we'll just read through them quickly. I want to have a lot of time today to take all of your questions. So this is sort of an outline, and then we're going to go into each one of these areas and discuss it with you a little bit. So one, to make sure everyone is heard through taking turns or speaking privately. This is about what you do in the coaching conversation with two or more clients. So number two, never take a side or appear to be siding with one person. Three, when necessary, be the voice of your clients with the purpose of elevating their communication. And you see Tony do this all the time in the Breakthrough episodes. Four, introduce a third option if working with two choices which are being negotiated. So in our own coaching, and I think as just part of being a strategic interventionist, a huge strength you bring to your coaching is the ability to be creative and flexible and to create an atmosphere of brainstorming and giving different options. Discussing without having to be right, you can throw out different creative solutions and create the environment with your clients that you're brainstorming together to find a third way or a fourth way or a fifth way. Then always end a session with a positive experience, whether it's a memory, a future plan of action, or an emotional purpose. It's important that every session you end specifically when you're working with more than two people ends in this positive mode because you're giving them the emotional clarity and the feeling that you want them to continue with after the session. So even if painful truths have been discussed or sometimes no agreement has been reached, whatever it is, you always want to end on that high emotional note.

As the coach, you have that unique ability, that power in the relationship to point out how important that person is. And that will help a resistant client to speak their point of view.

## [24:19 - 27:32]

You want to choose your battles wisely to be clear in a session about how you want to help the two or more people in the following hour or hours. And when you have a clear purpose, this will set the tone for the clients to want solutions. So, you want to encourage and complement honesty, emotions coming to the session. So, you want to find ways to elevate the couple through your positive observations. And you want to remember you want to see the good, you want to stay with your purpose and know that you can change today's experience of the relationship with this couple or family. So, this is a lot of this is about holding your space as a coach and setting the guidelines or the precedent for people so that they understand. People go into coaching sessions, they go, they think that it's going to be like old school ad therapy that you see in movies where, you know, they're going to be dragged in and psychoanalyzed or labeled or be patronized or, yeah, they have to talk about things that they don't want to talk about. And so, you know, the second step when you have two people involved is really about you taking the space and saying, listen, I'm here for you and I'm here for you. And be very confident that there is a goal and there is an objective and there's a shared purpose for them. So, for example, setting a goal as a coach for the session can be in the first five minutes telling the people you're working with that today your plan is to help both of them understand their own and the other's most important way to have fun. That's just a random example I'm throwing out there, but it could be about any need or any vehicle or any one thing like that. So sometimes you need to say over again what one person has said, and you need to say it in a way that it is heard more easily by their partner.

## [27:32 - 30:38]

So for example, a parent could have gone through what could sound to a teenager like a guilt trip, but the coach steps in and says, I'm hearing how afraid your dad is right now. Do you agree? Does he get really worried and afraid for you? And you can talk about the underlying emotion that the parent has and shift that communication from feeling like a way to make the teen, for example, feel guilty into a way for the teen to actually feel compassion for the parent or to help the parent speak from a place of their emotion instead of their demands, just as a quick example. Take a complaint and explain it as an expression of love and concern. Remind the couple of there, if you're working with a couple of their deepest, most truthful and loving desires, you could say something like, from seeing you, my guess is, or I imagine that. And saying things in a very non-arrogant way gives space for the couple to agree with you that you do understand them, that you do have it right. And I often find myself asking all the time, did I understand you correctly? Let me say what I think you mean, and please correct me if I'm wrong. And this is very different than putting words into the person's mouth. This is being part of the exploration between the two people. Remember always that most people want very simple things. They want love, respect, loyalty, honesty, and protection from their relationships. Some people want more certainty, some people want more growth, some people want more variety, in addition to the other five emotions. You can take any desire presented by your client and explain it through a need they have, and that way you make it much easier for the other person to understand what they're asking for and to be able to give it. So you're basically teaching the clients, for example, when that person says that to you, which sounds like a complaint or sounds like a guilt trip or whatever it is, really what you need to hear is, I want to be reminded that you love me, or I want some attention, I want to feel special. And it's important that you explain this without making the person feel bad that they want attention or that they want this type of love or connection. You need to let them know that it's good to want those things.

## [30:39 - 33:41]

It's good to express a way to give the other person a way that they can give that emotion to them easily. That this is what the secret is of people in successful relationships, whatever kind of relationship it is, they're able to communicate what they want, and they don't feel that the other person has to be a mind reader. Instead, let's say it's their birthday and they want to be told happy birthday, they call all their friends and say, hey, remember, it's my birthday. I wanted to talk to you. I wanted to hear your voice today. And that's, yeah, that's at the heart of being a person who creates the life experience they want is being able to ask for that experience. And that's a very powerful thing you can give your clients is teaching them to ask and create the relationship that gives them. And that's what they need. That's great. And the other thing that you always want to move people, when people are afraid, they tend to frame and experience communications very differently than when they feel like there's a feeling of good intentions. When you're the voice and you're translating for someone, you're translating them from a feeling of a frame that has to do with judgment and blame and fear, and you're moving it towards good intentions, needs, and communication. So, for instance, if someone says, you know, I always feel like this is, you know, and they complain and then by being the voice, you can help them find a well-intentioned way of looking at that. So, an example of a metaphor that I always like is when you're driving in a car and someone honks, we tend to have an angry response because, you know, we're irritated when someone honks. And we often think, like, oh, screw you, and people get really infallible in cars, right? And now that's based on the assumption that using the horn is somehow offensive to you or that their person is being injured. The truth is that the horn is the only instrument you really have in a car to get someone's attention. So that person may be trying to save your life. So, you know, when people get into the blame frame or they're afraid, basically, and angry, and they feel like the person is trying to hurt them, they're trying to pursue their own interests, or that they're enjoying hurting them, then they're creating, they're imagining that there's that intention behind the action and behind the communication. So when you're the voice and you're translating for the couple, what someone says, or you're putting, kind of putting words into their mouth sometimes, then you want to focus on the opposite, on the communication that is perceived as maybe as hurtful. How can that be looked as a need, or how can it be looked as an expression of good intention in a certain context?

And then, I guess this would be my style, is to go back, like, to the 30,000 elevation thing and say, you know what, you can offer like a concept that is a reframe. Like, you know, you guys are in the life stage of this, or you're struggling with this kind of thing when what's really going on is this, and you give it a different priority, and you give it a different context or scope. When you give it... you put it in a different scope like that, you say, for instance, this is a matter of a life... this is a matter of a need, or this is a matter of a life stage, or this is a matter of a work-life balance situation, then you refrained it in a way that... in a way that is neither taking sides with one or the other, and then both have to say, huh, what? And then when you reframe it that way, you are taking them away from the comfort zone of their fight, because now they have to figure out what this new frame, what this new meaning is. So that's the one way of looking at the third option. I think that everyone's got a different way of doing it. That tends to be my way of looking in terms of frameworks. And I think that's probably another type of third option that you guys would use. Well, I was remembering, Mark, this session that we had where the couple had a disagreement about wanting to never move, and the other wanting to live in another state.

## [43:11 - 46:16]

And creatively, we came up with a completely different option for them in terms of where they lived and how they worked. They didn't end up needing to use that third option, but both agreeing that that was actually possible, that so many different things were possible, and that created a much more nurturing environment for them to work on what really mattered, which was their life day to day. So you're really great at breaking. They'll give you an operational dilemma. Like, well, we can't live in both California and Oregon, and you'll say, actually, let's look at how you can. And you'll break that up and say, well, what if you went here and you did some work, and you'll offer a completely different worldview that is breaking up the dilemma that they thought they had. So that's the second way of presenting a third option. Like I said, the first one is, or one of them is reframing it. One of them is looking at the details, concretely detailed, and finding ways to make it work. Right. And when I do that, for example, my goal as a coach is not actually to have them follow my suggestion in any way. All I'm doing is modeling the ability to think creatively and create many different options that could satisfy both of their needs. So the actual advice I give is never the real intervention. It's an example of how to think and getting them into the frame of mind that they will create many different options in the spirit of working together and finding something that will make both happy. And that's really the best kind of pattern interrupt for two people in a disagreement, because it shows them you guys are, if you're thinking creatively, you wouldn't be fighting. Right. And so that leads right into this third point here that explain that to do what one person wants will not bring happiness to either person in the long run. So usually, most of the time, it seems that if one person just gives in their desire for the sake of the other in the long term, both, neither one are happy. So it's important to help them both win, both get their needs met. And then lastly here, explain you can help create a plan, decision, or choice, which makes both people happier, but first both must want to find this type of solution. So in this point, what we're saying is that it's very important that the two people are congruent that they want to have a solution, that to stay in an undecided place, to stay in a common, comfortable fight about something isn't where they want to be.

If you take on working with people that you may be concerned about, you make sure that there is an emergency session option for them. The other parent trap that people sometimes fall into is huge amounts of anxiety about the future for their child. because she was drinking in order to still have it, and now she's going, you know, if you're going to be so unreasonable, I'm not just going to give you everything you want. ## [1:25:49 - 1:28:49]

Then there can be the modality of the individual that plans exactly what she wants independently from him. And there can be the...
